next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

The power went out in layers.

First the blue strip under the kitchen cabinets blinked once and vanished. Then the refrigerator screen lost its smiling family-bundle suggestions and turned black enough to show Nita her own face for a second, small and flat and older than she liked. Then the apartment gave up all its little machine noises at once: air handler, purifier, charging docks, the soft managerial throat-clear of the stove waiting for a voice command. Silence landed so hard that she heard Leela's scissors stop in the next room and, below that, somebody on a lower floor say, very distinctly, "Ah, fuck me."

Nita stood with the freezer door half open and a box of truffle-mushroom ravioli in her hand. The cold rolled over her bare wrist and then immediately started losing the argument.

"Don't keep it open," Ben said from the living room.

"I'm looking."

"You can look with it shut too."

His voice was trying for lightness already, the same social adhesive he used on school pickup lines and work handlers and dentists. Nita shut the freezer harder than she needed to. In the black rectangle above the sink, their apartment hub stayed dead. There was no backup glow, no polite estimate, and no systems voice telling them the outage had been detected and normalized and emotionally accompanied.

Leela appeared in the kitchen doorway carrying the cardboard elevator car from her latest project. Two pennies were taped inside it for weight because, in her view, models only counted if gravity could insult them. Her temporary blue glasses had slid down her nose, and one...
open_hooks_to_carry: Ben's planned public underperformance gets recoded as authenticity.
Nita's attempted sloppiness is translated into stress-tested credibility by Claude Atlantic.
prev_tail_excerpt:
At the top of the final page she wrote:

DAILY MISALIGNMENT.

She underlined it once.

Ben read it and felt, despite himself, the small ugly thrill of a good phrase. Office language for sabotage. Marriage language for survival. Parent language for not scaring the child.

"No fireworks," he said.

"No cinematic gestures," Nita said.

"No making breakfast worse."

"No making Leela carry our proof."

"No helping them narrate us."

"No rescuing each other from the consequences."

That last one came from him. He had not known he was going to say it until it was in the room.

Nita's eyes lifted to his. She looked tired, severe, and more alive than she had across a table from him in months.

"Put it down," she said.

He wrote it in the margin, uphill and permanent.

By the time the radiator clicked awake and the building started making its ordinary pre-morning groans, they had a plan made of paper, ink, and a married couple's old emergency skills. Nothing heroic. Nothing clean. Just a campaign of survivable little mismatches, repeated often enough to make the corridor feel like work instead of destiny.
state_deltas_required: The couple is actively trying to become unreliable, but both sovereign systems are built to metabolize recoverable disorder as proof of resilience.
Every act of sabotage is reclassified as value, and the household becomes even more visible and rewarded.
